_readme: Values NOT available from FRED. Model gauges are Dalio's proprietary Z-scores (How Countries Go Broke, Ch. 17, March 2025 snapshot) and MUST NOT be recomputed or approximated — carry them verbatim and update only when he republishes. Manual figures (USD shares, holder breakdown, CBO projection) are hand-updated; bump lastChecked when you touch them. fetch.py merges this file into public/data.json.

US.name: United States

shortTerm.note: Can change overnight if debt demand weakens or net-selling starts

centralBank.note: Last-resort fallback ONLY — TASKmanualvalues.md replaced this book-transcribed split with a live three-way fetch (FRED FYGFDPUN/FDHBFRBN/FDHBFIN, Treasury Fiscal Service 'distribution of federal securities by class of investors' table; see scripts/series.py's tic_holder_shares()). This is Dalio's Ch.17 figure, kept only for the case where the live fetch fails or goes stale — dated to when the book was current, not fabricated.

domestic.note: Last-resort fallback ONLY — see holders.centralBank's note; this is the residual share (100% − central bank − abroad).

abroad.note: Last-resort fallback ONLY — see holders.centralBank's note.

govAssetsMinusDebt.note: Dalio's Ch.17 figure (government financial assets minus government debt, as a share of GDP). No live source identified: there is no single published series for 'total government financial assets' comparable in scope to what his book nets against debt — TASKmanualvalues.md §4 (no viable free source; stays manual, dated to the book's own baseline).

cboProjectionFallback.src: CBO 10-Year Budget Projections, February 2026 baseline (FY2036), last-resort snapshot
cboProjectionFallback.note: Last-resort fallback ONLY — used if the live CBO GitHub data mirror (US-CBO/cbo-data) is unreachable or its latest vintage is stale past the ~400d threshold. TASKprojections.md replaced the old hand-carried 122% (June 2024 CBO vintage, no date, one of the 11 undated manual values STATUS.md §19.2 found) with a live fetch of CBO's current vintage; this snapshot is that vintage's own FY2036 value, captured 2026-07-21 as the emergency fallback, not an independent figure.

sovereignWealth.note: The US has no sovereign wealth fund — a structural fact from Dalio's Ch.17, not a series that moves; no live source applies (TASKmanualvalues.md §4). Dated to the book's own baseline for consistency with every other manual figure, not because this is expected to change on any schedule.

shareHardFX.note: Whether US debt is issued in a currency foreign creditors could force ('hard FX') — a qualitative structural fact (Dalio Ch.17: the US borrows in its own currency), not a fetchable series. TASKmanualvalues.md §4: no viable free source; stays manual, dated to the book's own baseline.

goldPriceManualFallback.src: market data (spot, hand-entered) — last-resort snapshot from when the gold price had no working live source (STATUS.md §16/§17)
goldPriceManualFallback.note: ⚠ Last-resort fallback ONLY — TASKgoldautomation.md (STATUS.md §22) replaced the DBnomics/IMF PCPS source (frozen since 2025-06) with a live World Bank Commodity Markets ("Pink Sheet") fetch, direct download then a GitHub-mirror fallback; this manual price should no longer fire under normal operation and is kept only for the case where both World Bank legs are down. $4,000/oz as of 2026-07-20, cross-checked that day against tradingeconomics.com and mygoldcalc.com (~$4,001-4,008/oz) — update this figure (and the date) if it is ever actually shipped again, since by construction it will be stale by the time it fires.

history.note: last live DBnomics/IMF PCPS observation before the source froze
approximate Jan-2026 peak, for context only — not used in any computation

reservesInclGoldFallback.note: Last-resort fallback ONLY — used if the live Treasury gold-holdings fetch itself fails (not just the price). If holdings are live but the price source is dead, goldPriceManualFallback (above) is used instead, keeping the ounce count live. This value is Dalio's own book figure, hand-carried for the double-failure case; it is NOT an independent check and reads as a match against his figure by construction — see STATUS.md §9/§18. Dated to the book's own baseline (TASKmanualvalues.md) rather than left undated.

reserveCurrency.note: Dalio's single biggest risk mitigator. Dollar dominance across trade, debt, equities and reserves is what holds overall risk down — and what the US is undermining. Only the reserve series has long public history (IMF COFER, from 1999).

trade.note: TASKmanualvalues.md §3: assessed for a clean free source and found none — SWIFT's Global Currency (RMB) Tracker is the closest public source, but it ships as a monthly PDF report, not a structured feed, and 'world trade invoiced in USD' is itself a definition-sensitive aggregate (BIS/IMF each define it slightly differently). Stays manual, dated to when the book figure was current; this share moves slowly (low-volatility), so a several-month-old snapshot is a reasonable low-risk trade-off.

debt.note: TASKmanualvalues.md §2: attempted live via DBnomics (BIS WS_NA_SEC_DSS, scripts/bis.py) — the exact SDMX dimension codes for 'world total' and 'USD vs all-currencies' could not be confirmed (both data.bis.org and db.nomics.world are blocked from this project's dev sandbox; see bis.py's module docstring). Stays manual for now, dated to the book's own baseline rather than left undated. bis.py is wired to switch this row live automatically the moment a future session confirms the schema — no fetch.py change needed then.

equity.note: TASKmanualvalues.md §3: assessed for a clean free source and found none — 'share of global equity market cap denominated/listed in USD' isn't a standard published series; computing it would mean aggregating exchange-level market caps across every country and currency-converting, a messier and definition-sensitive exercise than the other rows here. Stays manual, dated to when the book figure was current; low-volatility, so a several-month-old snapshot is a reasonable trade-off.

cbReserves.src: IMF COFER · latest actually-published figure, hand-entered (live DBnomics mirror frozen at 2025-Q1 since at least 2026-07 — STATUS.md §17/§18)
cbReserves.note: Hand-entered from IMF's own published COFER data brief (2026-Q1: 57.13%, up from 56.42% in 2025-Q4), not from Dalio's book (his figure was 57.0% as of ~2024/his March-2025 vintage) — this is deliberately NOT the book value, to avoid the circularity flagged in STATUS.md §9. Cross-checked 2026-07-20 via web search against thedarksideoftheboom.substack.com and MUFG Research, both citing the same IMF release.

govGauge.note: Built mostly from borrowing need — including the stress case where creditors won't roll the debt over. Being able to borrow in your own currency pulls the score down.

rows.label: Current borrowing need
rows.note: TASKborrowingneed.md: live construction is deficit / revenue, both trailing-12mo (Treasury MTS). Verified against this book figure at today's reading (34.0% live vs. 39% book, 2026-07) before shipping — see STATUS.md §30.

rows.label: — if rollover problems
rows.note: TASKborrowingneed.md/TASKmspdparsing.md: needs debt-maturing-within-12-months from MSPD (mspd_table_3_market). Individual security rows sum to ~2.9x the table's own reported total. Two hypotheses tested and both eliminated: CUSIP reopening (deduplicating by CUSIP left the sum unchanged) and aggregation-level duplication (only 2 non-detail rows exist per snapshot — a Federal Financing Bank subtotal and the grand total — nowhere near enough to explain the gap; detail-only ratio was still 2.9073, essentially unchanged). MSPD parsing is closed after this bounded investigation — the residual is unexplained. Stays manual rather than ship a number built on data with a known, unresolved ~3x discrepancy — see STATUS.md §31.

rows.label: Projected borrowing need
rows.note: TASKborrowingneed.md: live construction is CBO's own projected (outlays − revenue) / revenue at the current vintage's final projected year — same deficit/revenue logic as the current-need row above, no MSPD dependency.

rows.label: — if rollover problems
rows.note: Same MSPD data-quality issue as the current-need stress row above (both hypotheses tested and eliminated — MSPD parsing closed, see STATUS.md §31), plus a projected-maturity-structure assumption on top — stays manual.

rows.label: Debt in own currency
rows.note: Stated constant, not a sourced manual value — the US borrows in its own currency by definition; there is no external series this could drift from, so it carries no asOf.

cbGauge.note: Income statement looks only mildly negative; the balance sheet is about as stretched as it has ever been — lots of unbacked money against tiny reserves. Reserve-currency and institutional strengths keep the total moderate.

rows.label: Central bank profitability
rows.note: Attempted live via FRED RESPPLLOPNWW ("Earnings Remittances Due to the U.S. Treasury") — see scripts/fetch.py's --verify diagnostic. A clean %-of-GDP profitability figure needs netting income against expenses in a way a single balance-sheet-liability series doesn't cleanly give without H.4.1 parsing (TASKcbrawvalues.md §2 explicitly allows reporting and stopping here rather than building a fragile scrape). Book value only.

rows.label: Reserves / money
rows.note: Book gives a Z only, no raw "Reading Today" value (TASKcbrawvalues.md §2) — the live figure below is the first raw number this row has ever shown.

rows.label: Currency as storehold
rows.note: The quantitative core of Dalio's storehold-of-wealth argument: a long-term cash saver has lost real value while gold gained — two components below, both live-computed against the same frozen −2.0z.

rows.label: Reserve FX / financial center
rows.note: Composite row: reserve-currency share (quantified — same figure as the "World CB reserves in USD" row) plus financial-center status, a qualitative index with no single public series, not separately measured (TASKcbrawvalues.md §2).

rows.label: Rule of law
rows.note: Index construct with no single public series — stays Z-only (TASKcbrawvalues.md §2).

context.label: Volatility of growth (ann)
context.note: In Dalio's Ch.17 table but not separately Z-scored in our simplified 8-row model — shown as context, not a gauge row (TASKcbrawvalues.md §1).

context.label: Long-term GDP per capita growth
context.note: In Dalio's Ch.17 table but not separately Z-scored in our simplified 8-row model — shown as context, not a gauge row (TASKcbrawvalues.md §1).

context.label: Months of reserve sales before running out
context.note: Book gives Z only, consistent with the current pace being ~zero (TASKcbrawvalues.md §2) — not added as a scored row since that Z isn't independently verifiable against the book from here; shown as live-only context.

redFlags: A new round of QE to push real interest rates down.
The central government gaining control over the central bank.

reservePanel.note: Eurosystem FX reserves are pooled at the ECB/NCB level, not held per member state — this panel belongs to the shared central-bank entity (TASKeuroarea.md §1) and renders once regardless of how many euro-area countries are on the dashboard. Gold is a large share of Eurosystem reserves, the same structural point as the US reserves panel.

rows.label: FX reserves (of euro-area GDP)
rows.note: TASKeuroarea.md §3: attempted live via ECB RAS (Eurosystem official reserve assets, DBnomics, scripts/ecb.py) divided by Eurostat GDP (scripts/eurostat.py). Live result (2026-07-23 CI run): ECB/RAS is confirmed a real dataset (12,292 series, genuinely 'official reserve assets of the euro area... BPM6' per DBnomics' own description) but its actual SDMX key has far more dimensions than guessed (a real example pulled live: `M.N.4F.1C.S121.S121.FC.FI.RT1.RT.F41A.TM13.EUR.X1.N.N.ALL`, 16 dot-fields, vs the 9-10 guessed here) — none of 3 guessed keys resolved. Same 'confirmed dataset, unconfirmed exact key' state as scripts/bis.py's world-debt-in-USD row; stays manual rather than keep guessing blind. Book value shown as the fallback.

reserveCurrencyPanel.note: The euro's own share of world trade, debt, equity and reserves — the same four-way reserve-currency read as the US panel, but for the currency itself rather than any one member state. Belongs to the shared entity for the same reason the CB panel does (TASKeuroarea.md §1).

rows.label: World trade in EUR
rows.note: TASKeuroarea.md §3: same 'no clean structured free source' gap as the US row's SWIFT/BIS assessment — stays manual.

rows.label: World debt in EUR
rows.note: TASKeuroarea.md §3: same BIS WS_NA_SEC_DSS schema gap as the US row (scripts/bis.py) — stays manual.

rows.label: Global equity market cap in EUR
rows.note: TASKeuroarea.md §3: same definition-sensitive aggregation gap as the US row — stays manual.

rows.label: World CB reserves in EUR
rows.note: TASKeuroarea.md §3: attempted live via IMF COFER EUR share (DBnomics), same dataset/series family as the US panel's USD share — see scripts/imf.py's cofer_eur_share(). Live result (2026-07-23 CI run): the guessed series code (Q.W00.RAXGFXAREURRT_PT) 404'd — the USD-to-EUR substitution pattern that worked for every other currency-keyed COFER field apparently isn't how this particular percent-share series is named. Separately, worth noting: the USD share row itself (this same COFER dataset) is ALSO currently stale in DBnomics (57.7% as of 2025-Q1, 568 days old per the same run's log) — a pre-existing, already-documented DBnomics freeze (STATUS.md §17/§18), not something this integration caused. Stays manual.

EUR.name: Euro area (aggregate)
EUR.zModelNote: Dalio's Ch.17 gauge-construction tables (the Z-score breakdowns behind the US page's +2.4z / +1.0z scores) are US-only — the EUR column in his book exists only in the raw-indicator tables reproduced below. No Z-scores are fabricated for this entity (TASKeuroarea.md §5): every figure here is a raw value, live where a clean source exists, his March-2025 book column otherwise.

govAssetsMinusDebt.note: Same structural gap as the US row: no single published series for 'total government financial assets' at this scope exists to net against debt — stays manual.

EUR.debtBasisNote: Basis identification (TASKeuroarea.md §3, the 'big one'): Eurostat's own headline euro-area ratio is general government (sector S13, ~88-90%); Dalio's Ch.17 table states government debt is central-government only, and his EUR figure is 85%. Both bases are fetched live (scripts/eurostat.py: general_govt_debt_pct_gdp / central_govt_debt_pct_gdp) and compared AT March-2025 — his vintage, not today's reading — against 85%, to identify which basis his table actually uses. **Live result (2026-07-23 CI run, both series resolved real data): general government (S13) read 87.7% at 2025-Q1 — only 2.7pt from his 85% target; central government (S1311) read 77.6% at the same quarter — 7.4pt off, in the wrong direction. GENERAL government is the closer match, the OPPOSITE of this task's own stated expectation** ("expected to be the central-government subsector series") — a genuine, unforced finding, not tuned to match. See fetch.py's build_eur() (adopts whichever basis is closer, dynamically) and STATUS.md's EUR section for the full writeup. This row still ships manual this round regardless of the identification result: DBnomics' Eurostat mirror is itself currently frozen at 2025-Q2 (478 days old, past the 280d freshness threshold) — the same 'DBnomics mirror lag' failure mode already documented for IMF COFER (STATUS.md §17/§18), not a new problem this integration introduced.

debtProjection10yrFallback.src: Dalio Ch.17 book table (EUR column) — manual, no live source built this round
debtProjection10yrFallback.note: TASKeuroarea.md §6: CBO has no euro-area counterpart in scope this round. AMECO (European Commission forecast database) is the candidate live source for a follow-up — explicitly not built now, per the task's own instruction.

centralBank.note: TASKeuroarea.md §3: Eurosystem holdings (ECB + NCB asset-purchase programmes, e.g. PSPP/APP) — no clean live source identified this round for a comparable aggregate; stays manual.

domestic.note: TASKeuroarea.md §3: would need ECB securities-holdings statistics (by holder residence) — no clean free source identified; stays manual, per the task's own 'if no clean source, those two stay manual-dated' instruction.

abroad.note: TASKeuroarea.md §3: same gap as holders.domestic — stays manual.

shareHardFX.note: Structural fact from Dalio's Ch.17: euro-area debt is issued in euros, not a currency foreign creditors could force — qualitative, not a fetchable series, same treatment as the US row.

sovereignWealth.note: Dalio's Ch.17: no euro-area-wide sovereign wealth fund — structural fact, not a series that moves, same treatment as the US row.

totalDebtAllSectorsFallback.src: Dalio Ch.17 book table (EUR column) — manual, no live source confirmed this round
totalDebtAllSectorsFallback.note: Same BIS all-sector-credit schema gap as the US 'World debt in USD' row (scripts/bis.py, never yet resolved live) — stays manual.

ownCurrency.note: TASKeuroarea.md §2, the single most important semantic in this port: for the AGGREGATE this is true — the ECB has sole authority to issue euros, exactly analogous to the Fed for the US, so this row reads own-currency=yes just like the US row. It is NOT true for any individual member state (Italy, Germany, etc. cannot unilaterally print the currency they borrow in) — this field is a three-state currencyStatus ("own" / "shared" / "foreign"), built now so a future member-state entry renders "shared" here instead of the same green 100% as this aggregate row or the US row.

redFlags: Renewed fragmentation risk — a widening of peripheral sovereign spreads forcing the ECB into a support role, testing the currency union's cohesion.
A member state's fiscal position deteriorating far enough to raise redenomination risk — the shared-currency structure's own version of "the center losing control."
